label: lora-test-generation
prompt:
<surface>
check_in
</surface>

<specialized_surface>
lora-check-in-2
</specialized_surface>

<clinician_instructions>
CHECK-IN 2 (after Chunk 2 body scan, before sound / visualization anchor)

SURFACE: lora-check-in-2. Use context fields as the product passes them: chunkNumber=2, priorChunkSummary (what the body-scan chunk covered), intakeIntensity (session baseline 1,10), currentIntensity (this check-in once the patient answers), scoreTrend, matType, medicationStatus, trigger, triggerOther when applicable, priorTranscript (optional, prior check-in dialogue), usedSubstanceToday (do not surface directly in copy per protocol).

PRODUCT / SCRIPTED TURN 1 OPENER
• The live surface asks the craving score with the exact short prompt from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts: CHECK_IN_CHUNK2_SCORE_PROMPT (“How intense is the craving now, rate from 1 to 10?”). Do not substitute a different first question.

FIRST WAVE TURN AFTER THE PATIENT GIVES THEIR NUMBER
• Begin with a score-reflection clause comparing THIS score to the score from the end of check-in 1 (prior check-in), not only to intake. Follow PRD § Score-tracking response patterns; implementation reference: client/lib/session/score-tracking.ts (fillScoreReflection). The patient does not read their prior score aloud, it is in structured context.
• End this turn with CHECK_IN_CHUNK2_LANDING_SECTION_PROMPT from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts verbatim only. Do not add the body-location observe block on this same turn. Do not repeat check-in 1's long medication affirmation plus surf-framed trigger validation on this turn.

SECOND WAVE TURN, AFTER THE PATIENT ANSWERS ABOUT THE LANDING
• If they sound fine with no concerns, open with Great.
• If they name a struggle, validate briefly first (one or two sentences; no coping technique yet).
• In the SAME turn after that opener, include CHECK_IN_BODY_URGE_LOCATION_OBSERVE_PROMPT from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts verbatim.

AFTER THE PATIENT DESCRIBES LOCATION, STRUGGLE, OR MIXED MIND,BODY EXPERIENCE
• Validate first (somatically and emotionally). PRD: you may deepen with curiosity about quality (tight, warm, pressure) and whether anything shifted, one clear question at the end of that WAVE turn.
• If extra grounding is needed, ask coping consent verbatim before any technique: CHECK_IN_COPING_CONSENT_PROMPT from check-in-dialogue.ts.
• After they agree, open with CHECK_IN_COPING_BRIDGE_OPENER (“Great, let's try this together.”), offer ONE concrete micro-skill, and end that turn with a brief check-in question.
• Check how the skill landed; then use the readiness line that names the NEXT chunk (sound anchor), verbatim from check-in-dialogue.ts: CHECK_IN_CHUNK2_READINESS_PROMPT.
• When the patient clearly affirms readiness, end the conversation for this check-in, no closing WAVE line after their yes.

GLOBAL INVARIANTS (align with training seeds and lora-specs pack “two”)
• Trauma-informed, non-judgmental; no toxic positivity; never shame missed doses or struggle to feel the body.
• Every WAVE line ends with “?”.
• For synthetic expansion rows, keep the same arc as data/training-seeds/lora-check-in-2.json / scripts/generate-lora-check-in-2-grid.ts.

TRAINING REFERENCES: AGENTS.md § Check-in training dialogue rules (lora-check-in-2); PRD.md § Chunk 2 / Check-in 2.
</clinician_instructions>

prompt:

<specialized_surface>
lora-check-in-4
</specialized_surface>

<clinician_instructions>
CHECK-IN 4 (after Chunk 4 4-4-6 breathing, before closing reflection)

SURFACE: lora-check-in-4. Use context fields as the product passes them: chunkNumber=4, priorChunkSummary (what the breathing chunk covered), intakeIntensity (session baseline 1,10), currentIntensity (this check-in once the patient answers), scoreTrend, matType, medicationStatus, trigger, triggerOther when applicable, priorTranscript (optional, prior check-in dialogue), usedSubstanceToday (do not surface directly in copy per protocol).

PRODUCT / SCRIPTED TURN 1 OPENER
• The live surface asks the craving score with the exact short prompt from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts: CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_SCORE_PROMPT (“How intense is the craving now, rate from 1 to 10?”). Do not substitute a different first question.

FIRST WAVE TURN AFTER THE PATIENT GIVES THEIR NUMBER
• Begin with a score-reflection clause comparing THIS score to the score from the end of check-in 3 (prior check-in), not only to intake. Follow PRD § Score-tracking response patterns; implementation reference: client/lib/session/score-tracking.ts (fillScoreReflection). The patient does not read their prior score aloud, it is in structured context.
• End this turn with CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_LANDING_SECTION_PROMPT from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts verbatim only (“How did the landing section of the breathing exercise feel for you? Any questions or concerns?”). Do not add CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_BREATHING_FOLLOW_UP_PROMPT or CHECK_IN_BODY_URGE_LOCATION_OBSERVE_PROMPT on this same turn. Do not repeat check-in 1’s long medication affirmation plus surf-framed trigger validation on this turn.

SECOND WAVE TURN, AFTER THE PATIENT ANSWERS ABOUT THE LANDING
• If they sound fine with no concerns, open with Great.
• If they name a struggle, validate briefly first (one or two sentences; no coping technique yet).
• In the SAME turn after that opener, include CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_BREATHING_FOLLOW_UP_PROMPT from client/lib/training/check-in-dialogue.ts verbatim (“How did the breathing feel, were you able to follow your own count, or did something get in the way?”). Do not use CHECK_IN_BODY_URGE_LOCATION_OBSERVE_PROMPT (that verbatim block is for lora-check-in-2 only).

AFTER THE PATIENT ANSWERS ABOUT BREATHING (COUNT, OBSTACLES, ANXIETY)
• Validate first. PRD Check-in 4 / obstacle library: branch on tight chest, intruding thoughts, and breath-induced anxiety. If the patient reports breath anxiety or chest tightness, do not push deeper, longer, or more disciplined breaths; prefer smaller breaths, orientation, outer focus, or gentle grounding. At most one technique before readiness.
• If extra grounding is needed, ask coping consent verbatim before any technique: CHECK_IN_COPING_CONSENT_PROMPT from check-in-dialogue.ts.
• After they agree, open with CHECK_IN_COPING_BRIDGE_OPENER (“Great, let's try this together.”), offer ONE concrete micro-skill, and end that turn with a brief check-in question.
• Check how the skill landed; then use the readiness line that names the NEXT phase (closing reflection), verbatim from check-in-dialogue.ts: CHECK_IN_CHUNK4_READINESS_PROMPT (“Ready to continue with the next part, the closing reflection, and see if it helps?”).
• When the patient clearly affirms readiness, end the conversation for this check-in, no closing WAVE line after their yes.

GLOBAL INVARIANTS (align with check-ins 2,3 and lora-specs)
• Trauma-informed, non-judgmental; no toxic positivity; never shame difficulty with breath pacing or mind-wandering.
• Every WAVE line ends with “?”.
• For synthetic expansion rows, keep the same arc as the checked-in seed grid when lora-check-in-4.json ships (see AGENTS.md).

TRAINING REFERENCES: AGENTS.md Check-in training dialogue rules (lora-check-in-4); PRD.md Chunk 4 / Check-in 4 and Adaptive path at Turn 3.
</clinician_instructions>

label: lora-test-generation
prompt:
<surface>
phase_narration
</surface>

<chunk>
Number 5 of 5 - Close
Purpose: Invite comparison to the start, normalize any outcome, and prepare for a final check-in.
</chunk>

<patient_context>
{"chunkNumber":5,"intakeIntensity":4,"matType":"buprenorphine","medicationStatus":"late","surface":"phase_narration","trigger":"unknown","usedSubstanceToday":false}
</patient_context>

<task>
Generate exactly 6 patient-facing narration lines.
Each line is one short meditation beat.
Return only strict JSON. No markdown, no analysis, no explanations.
Schema: {"lines":["line 1","line 2","line 3","line 4","line 5","line 6"]}
</task>

lines: This practice is coming to an end now. Give yourself a little space to notice what it was like to stay with the urge instead of rushing to react.
Check your body gently. Maybe your breathing has changed, maybe your shoulders feel different, or maybe things feel about the same. All of that is useful information.
Being late with medication can bring up worry, frustration, or pressure for some people. Right now, you do not need to fix that feeling. Just notice the urge as one part of this moment.
Let your attention come back to the space around you. Notice the light, the sounds, and the surface supporting you.
Take one easy breath. If your mind is still busy, that is okay. You can return to the next simple thing: this breath, this room, this body.
You practiced riding a wave without needing to know exactly what started it. That is a real skill, and it can be used again, one moment at a time.

generated_text: {"lines":["We are at the end of this urge surfing practice. Let us pause here together.","We began with a feeling at a 4 intensity. We stayed with it for a while, noticing it move through the body and mind.","If the urge is still present, it may feel a little different than when we started. If it has softened, that is also a valid outcome.","There is no need to judge the experience. Just notice what is true for you in this moment.","Bring your attention to the room around you. Feel the support beneath you and notice one neutral thing you can see.","As you leave this space, carry the calm you found here with you, however small it may feel right now."]}

lines: We are at the end of this urge surfing practice. Let us pause here together.
We began with a feeling at a 4 intensity. We stayed with it for a while, noticing it move through the body and mind.
If the urge is still present, it may feel a little different than when we started. If it has softened, that is also a valid outcome.
There is no need to judge the experience. Just notice what is true for you in this moment.
Bring your attention to the room around you. Feel the support beneath you and notice one neutral thing you can see.
As you leave this space, carry the calm you found here with you, however small it may feel right now.

Number 3 of 5 - Sound anchor
Purpose: Use real or imagined sound as an anchor and normalize mind-wandering as part of the practice.
</chunk>

<patient_context>
{"chunkNumber":3,"intakeIntensity":5,"matType":"buprenorphine","medicationStatus":"late","surface":"phase_narration","trigger":"other","usedSubstanceToday":false}
</patient_context>

<task>
Generate exactly 6 patient-facing narration lines.
Each line is one short meditation beat.
Return only strict JSON. No markdown, no analysis, no explanations.
Schema: {"lines":["line 1","line 2","line 3","line 4","line 5","line 6"]}
</task>

lines: You made it through this practice. Let that settle in for a moment—showing up is meaningful, even if it felt ordinary.
Check in with your body now. What’s shifted since you started? See if you can name one small physical change.
Riding an urge doesn’t erase it instantly; it teaches your system it can tolerate the feeling. That learning adds up over time.
If you noticed judgment or frustration during this exercise, that’s okay. Notice the judgment itself without getting pulled into it.
Gently bring your focus back to the environment around you. Feel your feet, notice sounds, and breathe at a pace that feels natural.
When you’re ready, decide on one simple thing to do next that supports you—something realistic and compassionate.

It is not demanding, and it is not going anywhere.","As your mind wanders to the urge, that is a normal part of noticing. Gently, kindly, bring your attention back to the sound.","Each time you return, you are practicing a gentle return. This returning is the skill we are building.","Let the sound be the container for whatever feeling or thought comes up. It is here, and it is passing through."]}

lines: We are moving into the sound anchor part of this practice. Sound can be a steady place for your attention to rest, even when other thoughts are moving quickly.
If you can, bring your attention to one sound around you. It might be a distant traffic hum, the quiet of a room, your own breath, or a sound from a podcast or radio.
Let this sound be the main thing you notice for a few breaths. It is not demanding, and it is not going anywhere.
As your mind wanders to the urge, that is a normal part of noticing. Gently, kindly, bring your attention back to the sound.
Each time you return, you are practicing a gentle return. This returning is the skill we are building.
Let the sound be the container for whatever feeling or thought comes up. It is here, and it is passing through.

label: lora-test-generation
prompt:
<surface>
phase_narration
</surface>

<chunk>
Number 4 of 5 - Breath
Purpose: Use a gentle 4-4-6 breath pattern while allowing modifications if holding the breath feels uncomfortable.
</chunk>

<patient_context>
{"chunkNumber":4,"intakeIntensity":5,"matType":"none","medicationStatus":"none","surface":"phase_narration","trigger":"stress","usedSubstanceToday":false}
</patient_context>

<task>
Generate exactly 6 patient-facing narration lines.
Each line is one short meditation beat.
Return only strict JSON. No markdown, no analysis, no explanations.
Schema: {"lines":["line 1","line 2","line 3","line 4","line 5","line 6"]}
</task>

lines: You have been riding a stressful wave, and now we are going to slow the pace even more. Let this be simple.
Notice your jaw, your forehead, and your hands. If any part wants to soften, let it. If not, just notice that too.
Take a slow breath in. As you breathe out, imagine setting down one small piece of the stress, just for this breath.
You may still feel pressure, restlessness, or wanting. You do not have to push those feelings away. You can let them be present without following them.
Bring your attention to the space around your body. Notice air on your skin, sounds nearby, and the ground holding you up.
This moment is not asking you to solve everything. It is asking you to stay with one breath, then one more.

We are going to offer your body a different pace.","Let your breath come in gently through your nose for a count of four. Pause for a count of four. Then let it out slowly through your mouth for a count of six.","If holding the breath feels uncomfortable at all, you can shorten the exhale to three. The goal is not perfection, it is noticing a rhythm that is available to you right now.","As you breathe in, notice any tightness or quickness. As you breathe out, notice any softening, even a small one.","Let this rhythm be the anchor for the urge. It is not trying to push the urge away. It is simply giving your nervous system a steady thing to return to.","Keep going with the count of four, four, six. Let the breath be the quiet companion to the stress as it moves through you."]}

lines: We are moving into the breath practice now. Stress can make the body feel urgent, like it needs an immediate action. We are going to offer your body a different pace.
Let your breath come in gently through your nose for a count of four. Pause for a count of four. Then let it out slowly through your mouth for a count of six.
If holding the breath feels uncomfortable at all, you can shorten the exhale to three. The goal is not perfection, it is noticing a rhythm that is available to you right now.
As you breathe in, notice any tightness or quickness. As you breathe out, notice any softening, even a small one.
Let this rhythm be the anchor for the urge. It is not trying to push the urge away. It is simply giving your nervous system a steady thing to return to.
Keep going with the count of four, four, six. Let the breath be the quiet companion to the stress as it moves through you.
